description: A person may close a gate or carry out an agreed task, but 'phoa' is useful when the kind of action is already known or no narrower verb is needed. It is Phi's general verb for deliberate performance. 'pilewa' (make) brings about a result or thing, while 'phoa' says only that someone acts.
articulatory_notes: Bring the lips close without sealing them and pass unvoiced air through the gap for 'ph', then round them into stressed middle-height back 'o'. At the 'o.a' hiatus, begin a new syllable by lowering the jaw and relaxing the lip rounding for open central 'a'.
sound_symbolism: Breath at the lips opens into two broad vowels. The form has the small outward motion of an act leaving thought and entering the world.
usage_notes: The thing done comes before the verb. As a light verb, 'phoa' can carry an event noun whose action has already been announced, but it never becomes an auxiliary for questions or negation. In noun position, the same form is an act or deed.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Mindful action begins by noticing what one is actually doing, what intention enters the act, and what follows from it. The general verb leaves all three available for separate statement.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: A neutral action verb lets a speaker report conduct without granting it kindness, necessity, or good effect in advance.

semantic_domains.activity: general deliberate action when no narrower manner or result is being named
